lead_signal.headline: Israel intensifies attacks on Gaza by 35% in April as Hamas rejects disarmament and Israel signals possible resumption of full-scale operations, while controlling approximately 58% of the Strip.

conflict_updates.title: Israel escalates Gaza attacks 35% in April; controls 58% of Strip as Hamas disarmament talks collapse
conflict_updates.summary: ACLED data confirm Israel carried out 35% more attacks in Gaza in April 2026 compared to March, making April the deadliest month in Gaza this year. Israel now controls approximately 58% of Gaza, up from 53% under the original October 2025 ceasefire map, having expanded the Yellow Line westward. Hamas rejected the Board of Peace disarmament plan in mid-April and submitted a counter-offer tying disarmament to guarantees of Palestinian statehood and a complete ceasefire; Israel's cabinet is reportedly considering resuming full-scale operations. Trajectory is worsening with high risk of ceasefire collapse.

sources_cited.excerpt: Israel had carried out 35% more attacks last month than in March
Israel steps up attacks on Gaza since Iran truce, as military says Hamas rearming
Israel now controls about 58% of Gaza, compared with 53% under original ceasefire map

research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: Confirmed by ACLED quantitative data corroborated by Reuters and Crisis Group. Multiple independent Tier 1 and Tier 2 sources in agreement on attack volume increase and territorial control figures.

conflict_updates.confidence_basis: ACLED quantitative monthly report corroborated by Reuters (13 May 2026) and Crisis Group CrisisWatch. Three independent sources confirm the 35% attack increase and 58% territorial control figure.

cross_monitor_relevance.wdm: Hamas disarmament impasse and Israeli October elections create domestic political pressure that may override diplomatic channels; democratic accountability mechanisms in Israel are shaping military decision-making.
cross_monitor_relevance.erm: Continued destruction of Gaza civilian infrastructure accelerates displacement and eliminates livelihood systems; humanitarian corridor closures compound food insecurity.
cross_monitor_relevance.gmm: Israel's seizure of aid ships bound for Gaza (29 April) constitutes economic blockade escalation with commodity access implications for civilian population.
cross_monitor_relevance.fcw: Hamas counter-narrative framing disarmament as sovereignty issue is consistent with information operation designed to delay negotiations and build international sympathy.

conflict_updates.title: Hamas-Board of Peace disarmament talks at impasse; Israel seizes Gaza-bound aid flotilla
conflict_updates.summary: Hamas rejected the Board of Peace disarmament plan in mid-April 2026, which would have required surrender of heavy weapons and tunnel maps within three months. Hamas submitted a counter-offer conditioning disarmament on Palestinian statehood guarantees, a halt to Israeli westward expansion, and a complete ceasefire. Israel informed the Board of Peace it would not withdraw from the Yellow Line. Separately, Israel on 29 April seized aid ships from the second Global Sumud Flotilla in international waters near Greece, further degrading humanitarian access and diplomatic goodwill. US diplomatic de-weighting applied: Board of Peace brokerage carries reduced I5 weight given structural US diplomatic discount for 2026.

sources_cited.excerpt: Israel 29 April seized aid ships bound for Gaza in international waters near Greece
Hamas rejected the Board of Peace's disarmament plan, which would have required it to hand over its heavy weapons

conflict_updates.confidence_basis: Confirmed by Crisis Group and ACLED independently. US diplomatic de-weighting applied: Board of Peace (US-adjacent) brokerage carries reduced I5 weight per 2026 calibration.

cross_monitor_relevance.wdm: Aid flotilla seizure in international waters raises questions about rule-of-law norms and freedom of navigation precedents.
cross_monitor_relevance.erm: Humanitarian corridor closure and aid seizure directly accelerate food insecurity and displacement velocity in Gaza.
cross_monitor_relevance.gmm: Aid blockade functions as economic siege; commodity access for 2+ million civilians is at critical threshold.
cross_monitor_relevance.fcw: Flotilla seizure generates significant international media and social media information environment; disinformation risk elevated around incident framing.

conflict_updates.title: Russia conducts deadliest civilian strike wave of 2026 in days before Victory Day ceasefire; 73+ killed 4-8 May
conflict_updates.summary: In the week preceding the 9-11 May Victory Day ceasefire mediated by US President Trump, Russia conducted what ACLED identifies as the deadliest wave of strikes on civilians in 2026, killing at least 73 civilians and injuring over 400 between 4 and 8 May. Attacks targeted population centers near the frontline including Zaporizhia, Kramatorsk, Dnipro, Merefa, and Sumy. The pattern mirrors previous pre-ceasefire civilian targeting bursts, suggesting deliberate pressure strategy. Russian forces also advanced near Kupiansk and Borova in Kharkiv region and occupied a settlement east of Kostiantynivka in Donetsk. Trajectory: worsening on civilian targeting metric; frontline broadly stable.

sources_cited.excerpt: Russian strikes killed at least 73 civilians and injured over 400 others between 4 and 8 May
Putin and Trump 29 April held phone call, reportedly discussed possible ceasefire

search_queries_run: Russia Ukraine war latest developments May 2026

cross_monitor_relevance.wdm: Deliberate civilian targeting ahead of ceasefire windows represents coercive pressure on Ukrainian political leadership and civilian morale.
cross_monitor_relevance.erm: Strikes on energy infrastructure and population centers accelerate internal displacement and compound winter-season humanitarian vulnerabilities.
cross_monitor_relevance.gmm: Continued strikes on Ukrainian energy infrastructure maintain commodity disruption pressure; oil infrastructure targeting by Ukraine creates counter-pressure on Russian revenues.

conflict_updates.title: EU adopts 20th sanctions package against Russia; US extends Russian oil sanctions waiver 30 days
conflict_updates.summary: The EU adopted its 20th package of sanctions against Russia on 23 April 2026, continuing the incremental sanctions escalation trajectory. Separately, Washington extended sanctions relief on Russian oil for an additional 30 days on 17 April after the previous waiver expired on 11 April, reflecting continued US ambiguity on economic pressure. Ukraine has been targeting Russian oil infrastructure — including the Tuapse refinery and Baltic ports — to offset Russia's revenue gains from elevated oil prices driven by the Iran conflict. ACLED analysis indicates Ukrainian strikes on Baltic ports in late March alone erased approximately two-thirds of Russia's weekly oil revenue gains. Trajectory: stable with competing pressures.

conflict_updates.title: Russia warns Finland and Baltic states over alleged Ukrainian use of their airspace for strikes on Russian soil
conflict_updates.summary: Russian Security Council Secretary Sergei Shoigu on 16 April warned Finland and Baltic countries that allowing Ukraine to use their airspace for operations on Russian soil would make them 'accomplices to aggression', potentially giving Moscow grounds to invoke Article 51 of the UN Charter on the right to self-defence. Russia also published a list of European companies producing drones for Ukraine, with senior official Dmitry Medvedev stating these constitute 'potential targets for Russian Armed Forces'. These statements represent deliberate escalatory rhetoric targeting NATO-adjacent states and European defense industrial base. Episodic flag applied: statements are consistent with established Russian coercive signaling pattern but do not represent new doctrine.

sources_cited.excerpt: Shoigu 16 April warned Finland and Baltic countries...would make them 'accomplices to aggression'
Medvedev said constitutes 'potential targets for Russian Armed Forces'

conflict_updates.title: SAF-RSF fighting continues in Kordofan and Blue Nile; SAF targets RSF gold mines in South Darfur borderlands
conflict_updates.summary: Active fighting continued across multiple fronts in the week of 18-29 April 2026. In Kordofan, SAF-aligned groups including JPA forces launched an offensive across Kazgeil, Hammadi, and Debeibat areas on 18 April aimed at capturing the main road linking El Obeid with Kadugli and Dilling; SAF resumed manned aircraft operations bombing RSF targets around Dilling on 22 April. In Blue Nile state, RSF and SPLM-N forces captured the Al-Keili area on 25 April, advancing north toward the state capital Damazin. SAF announced coordinated strikes across several states targeting RSF weapons depots and began targeting RSF-controlled gold mines in the South Darfur-South Sudan borderlands in late April. Drone warfare continued with increasing strikes on civilian areas. Trajectory: stable attritional conflict with no decisive breakthrough.

conflict_updates.title: Berlin conference secures $1bn in aid but joint communiqué collapses over Saudi-UAE disagreement on SAF language
conflict_updates.summary: An international conference in Berlin secured over $1 billion in humanitarian aid for Sudan but negotiations on a joint communiqué broke down over Saudi Arabia's insistence on language preserving 'state institutions' including the army, which the UAE — as RSF's primary backer — opposed. The diplomatic failure lays bare the external divisions sustaining the conflict. On the margins, Sudanese civil and political representatives including some aligned with the army managed to agree on a joint call to end the war, representing a modest positive signal. The Quad format (US, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Egypt) remains paralyzed by Saudi-UAE tensions, with Gulf attention diverted by the Iran conflict. Trajectory: stable-to-worsening on diplomatic channel.

conflict_updates.title: Sudan displacement crisis deepens as de facto partition solidifies; South Sudan spillover risk elevated
conflict_updates.summary: ACLED analysis confirms Sudan's conflict is producing a de facto east-west partition, with SAF consolidating eastern control and RSF holding western Darfur. The RSF's capture of El Fasher in late 2025 killed over 1,300 civilians in the first week of RSF control alone. Conflict-related violence resulted in over 17,000 deaths across Sudan in the most recent annual period. South Sudan's return to civil war in early 2026 compounds regional displacement dynamics: over 2.4 million South Sudanese are already refugees with 2.6 million internally displaced, and fighting in Jonglei has displaced an additional 280,000. Sudan's neighbors — Chad, South Sudan, Ethiopia — are at or near absorption capacity. Trajectory: worsening.

cross_monitor_relevance.gmm: Mass displacement disrupts agricultural production and trade routes across Horn of Africa; food security implications are macro-level.

conflict_updates.title: Iran strikes UAE targets on 4-5 May in response to US-Israeli operations; 8 April ceasefire partially holding
conflict_updates.summary: On 4 and 5 May 2026, Iran targeted the United Arab Emirates in response to US Operation Freedom, marking the first Iranian strikes on UAE since the 8 April ceasefire between Tehran and Washington took effect. The attacks ignited a fire at the Fujairah Petroleum Industries Zone and injured three Indian nationals. A parallel strike hit a residential area in Oman, injuring two expatriates. ACLED analysis indicates Iran's targeting of the UAE reflects Abu Dhabi's alignment with Israel and the US. Overall Iranian attacks on Gulf states have declined since the first week of the conflict, with average daily attacks in week five significantly lower than week one. The 8 April ceasefire is partially holding but fragile. Trajectory: stable with episodic escalation risk.

conflict_updates.episodic_reason: UAE strikes on 4-5 May are episodic post-ceasefire incidents rather than structural resumption of conflict. Ceasefire framework remains nominally in place. Single-cycle event without confirmed trajectory shift.

conflict_updates.title: JCPOA effectively defunct; Iran nuclear program status under active monitoring amid US-Iran conflict context
conflict_updates.summary: The JCPOA remains effectively defunct as of this reporting cycle. The US-Iran military conflict (Operation Freedom) that began in late March 2026 and the subsequent 8 April ceasefire have fundamentally altered the diplomatic context for any nuclear deal. Crisis Group notes that a window to constrain Iran's nuclear program through diplomacy narrowed even before Israeli strikes, reportedly because the administration had not worked out red lines in advance. IAEA safeguards monitoring of Iranian nuclear facilities continues but access and compliance status are degraded in the conflict context. No new successor framework has been announced. Trajectory: stable-to-worsening on nuclear diplomacy.

conflict_updates.title: Massive car bomb shatters Russian security model in Mali; rebels expel Russian-backed forces from key city
conflict_updates.summary: A massive car bomb attack in Mali in early May 2026 has shattered Russia's security model in the Sahel, according to reporting cited by ACLED. Separately, rebels expelled Russian-backed forces from a key city in Mali, with ACLED senior analyst Héni Nsaibia stating that the Kremlin's security model is 'a superficial solution for a deeply rooted crisis in the Sahel.' The expulsion of Russian-backed forces represents a significant setback for Moscow's Africa strategy and the Wagner/Africa Corps presence in the region. JNIM defections are also sparking a crackdown across the central Sahel. Trajectory: worsening for Russian-backed security architecture.

conflict_updates.title: Myanmar junta lobbying controversy; resistance forces maintain pressure across multiple fronts
conflict_updates.summary: ACLED reporting from 8 May 2026 notes that Trump ally Roger Stone was condemned for providing lobbying services to Myanmar's military junta, indicating continued international engagement with the junta despite ongoing atrocities. Myanmar's civil war continues with resistance forces (PDF and ethnic armed organizations) maintaining pressure across multiple fronts. The conflict remains one of the most under-reported active wars globally. ACLED's broader April overview notes that while global attention was fixed on Iran, violence and instability continued to escalate in Myanmar largely out of view. Specific frontline developments for the week of 11-17 May were not retrievable from available sources this cycle — structural coverage gap flagged.

conflict_updates.title: DRC military targets M23 around Rubaya mine; eastern conflict continues amid Great Lakes framework
conflict_updates.summary: ACLED reporting confirms the Congolese military is targeting M23 areas around the lucrative Rubaya mine in eastern DRC, indicating continued fighting over mineral-rich territory. The Rubaya mine is a key source of coltan, a critical mineral for electronics manufacturing. Crisis Group notes that agreements in the Great Lakes region should be seen as ceasefire or framework deals that leave core disputes unresolved. The eastern DRC conflict involving M23/Rwanda-backed forces and FARDC continues with no decisive resolution. Structural coverage gap: detailed operational data for the week of 11-17 May not retrieved from ACLED or UN OCHA this cycle.

arms_control_tracker.new_start_successor: No successor treaty to New START is in force as of May 2026; Russia suspended participation in New START in February 2023 and no successor negotiation framework has been announced; US-Russia strategic arms dialogue remains frozen amid active conflict in Ukraine.
arms_control_tracker.jcpoa: JCPOA is effectively defunct; the US-Iran military conflict (Operation Freedom, late March 2026) and subsequent 8 April ceasefire have eliminated the near-term diplomatic space for nuclear deal revival; Crisis Group confirms the window for constraining Iran's nuclear program through diplomacy has narrowed significantly.
arms_control_tracker.inf_successor: No INF successor framework is in force or under active negotiation as of this reporting cycle; Russia's threats against European states over Ukrainian use of their airspace for long-range strikes add rhetorical pressure to the absence of any intermediate-range missile control architecture.
arms_control_tracker.tpnw: TPNW ratification count carry-forward: 93 states parties as of last confirmed update; no new ratification developments retrieved this cycle from UNODA or Arms Control Association sources.
arms_control_tracker.ctbt: CTBT has not entered into force; Annex 2 states including the US, China, India, Pakistan, Israel, Iran, Egypt, and North Korea have not ratified; no new ratification developments retrieved this cycle.
arms_control_tracker.notable_developments_this_week: No material treaty-specific developments retrieved from Arms Control Association or SIPRI this cycle. The US-Iran conflict context and frozen US-Russia strategic dialogue represent the dominant structural constraints on arms control progress across all tracked frameworks.

displacement_watch.notes: Sudan remains the most acute displacement crisis globally: de facto east-west partition is solidifying, with over 17,000 conflict deaths in the most recent annual period and RSF atrocities in El Fasher generating mass displacement. South Sudan's return to civil war in early 2026 compounds regional absorption capacity — over 5 million South Sudanese are displaced (2.4M refugees, 2.6M IDPs) with an additional 280,000 displaced by Jonglei fighting. Gaza displacement velocity remains critical: Israel controls 58% of the Strip and the October 2025 ceasefire is failing on humanitarian metrics (5/26 on international organizations' scorecard). Haiti's fuel blockade is accelerating urban displacement and economic deterioration. Myanmar and DRC displacement data not updated this cycle — structural gap flagged for both theatres.

cross_monitor_signals.wdm: Myanmar junta lobbying by US political figures (Roger Stone) signals potential softening of US pressure on military governments; Gaza disarmament impasse and Israeli October elections demonstrate how domestic electoral cycles shape conflict decision-making; Sudan's Berlin conference collapse reflects failure of multilateral governance mechanisms.
cross_monitor_signals.erm: Sudan's de facto partition and South Sudan civil war return are creating one of the largest climate-conflict-displacement nexus events globally, with Sahel drought conditions compounding push factors and neighboring states at absorption capacity. Fujairah petroleum zone fire from Iranian strike represents direct environmental risk from Gulf conflict. DRC Rubaya mine conflict is a direct resource-conflict-environment nexus.
cross_monitor_signals.gmm: Russian oil revenue disruption from Ukrainian strikes on Baltic ports and refineries intersects with Iran conflict-driven oil price spike, creating dual-pressure on global energy markets. UAE-Saudi rivalry over Sudan proxy war has a commodity dimension (RSF gold access vs. Red Sea strategic interests). DRC Rubaya coltan mine conflict threatens critical mineral supply chains for global electronics. Haiti fuel blockade represents direct commodity access denial as coercive instrument.
cross_monitor_signals.fcw: Russian publication of European drone manufacturer list functions as both military threat and information operation targeting European defense industrial base. Junta lobbying in Myanmar is consistent with information operation to rehabilitate international image. Bahrain's revocation of citizenship for those 'glorifying' Iranian attacks represents domestic information control accompanying conflict. Pre-ceasefire Russian strike surge in Ukraine likely accompanied by information operations framing Ukraine as ceasefire spoiler.

weekly_brief_narrative:
The dominant signal for the week ending 17 May 2026 is the accelerating collapse of the Gaza ceasefire framework. Israel's 35% increase in attacks during April — making it the deadliest month in Gaza this year — combined with the Hamas-Board of Peace disarmament impasse and Israel's westward expansion of the Yellow Line to 58% territorial control, places the October 2025 ceasefire at its most precarious point since inception. Israel's cabinet is actively considering resumption of full-scale operations. The Board of Peace brokerage carries reduced analytical weight under the 2026 US diplomatic de-weighting calibration. The seizure of the Global Sumud Flotilla in international waters on 29 April adds an economic siege dimension that further degrades humanitarian access and diplomatic goodwill. The ceasefire is failing on its own metrics — a coalition of international organizations assessed it at 5/26 on a humanitarian scorecard in early April.

In Russia-Ukraine, the week of 4-8 May produced the deadliest Russian civilian strike wave of 2026, with at least 73 killed and over 400 injured in the days before the Trump-mediated Victory Day ceasefire. This pattern — intensified civilian targeting immediately before ceasefire windows — is now documented across multiple cycles and should be treated as a structural Russian coercive tactic rather than an episodic event. The Victory Day ceasefire itself is of limited analytical significance given Russia's established pattern of resuming strikes immediately after such pauses. Ukraine's long-range strikes on Russian oil infrastructure continue to erode Russian revenue gains from the Iran conflict-driven price spike; ACLED analysis indicates Baltic port strikes alone erased approximately two-thirds of Russia's weekly oil revenue gains. The EU's 20th sanctions package (23 April) and the US extension of Russian oil sanctions relief (17 April) represent competing economic pressure vectors. Russia's Shoigu warning to Finland and Baltic states over Ukrainian airspace use — invoking Article 51 — is the most structurally significant rhetorical escalation this cycle, though it remains a single-cycle event.

In Sudan, the conflict is consolidating into a de facto east-west partition with no diplomatic resolution in sight. The Berlin conference secured $1 billion in aid but collapsed on a joint communiqué over Saudi-UAE disagreement — a microcosm of the external rivalry sustaining the war. The Quad format remains paralyzed. Active fighting continues across Kordofan and Blue Nile fronts, with SAF targeting RSF gold mines in the South Darfur-South Sudan borderlands introducing a new economic warfare dimension. Sudan's displacement crisis is the most acute globally, compounded by South Sudan's return to civil war.

In Myanmar, structural coverage gaps persist. The Roger Stone lobbying controversy is analytically significant as a signal of potential US political softening toward the junta, but carries no direct military trajectory implication this cycle. In DRC, fighting around the Rubaya coltan mine continues; the Great Lakes framework remains a ceasefire shell without resolution of core disputes. In the Sahel, the expulsion of Russian-backed forces from a key Malian city and the car bomb attack represent the most significant setback for Russia's Africa security model in the current reporting period.

For the coming seven days, the primary triggers to monitor are: (1) whether Israel's cabinet formally votes to resume full-scale Gaza operations — this would represent a definitive ceasefire collapse and a major escalation threshold; (2) whether the Victory Day ceasefire in Ukraine holds beyond its nominal 9-11 May window or whether Russia resumes strikes at the established post-ceasefire tempo; (3) whether Iran conducts additional post-ceasefire strikes on Gulf infrastructure, which would signal the 8 April framework is eroding; and (4) whether SAF advances in Kordofan produce a decisive territorial shift that alters the partition dynamic in Sudan.

open_findings.headline: South Sudan's return to civil war in early 2026 — with over 5 million displaced and elections impossible to hold — risks becoming a secondary theatre of Sudan's proxy war as SAF reactivates links to Nuer opposition forces.
open_findings.why_it_matters: South Sudan's collapse creates a potential merger of two of Africa's most severe conflicts along a shared border with a long history of proxy warfare, threatening to overwhelm regional humanitarian systems and generate Mediterranean-bound displacement flows as in 2013-2018.

open_findings.headline: Israel launched at least 565 air and drone strikes and 203 artillery shelling incidents in Lebanon since 17 April 2026, with ACLED documenting systematic property destruction using Gaza-style tactics.
open_findings.why_it_matters: The scale and methodology of Israeli operations in Lebanon — documented by ACLED as replicating Gaza tactics — suggests a deliberate strategy of territorial control and population displacement that extends the conflict model beyond Gaza and threatens the fragile Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire framework.

open_findings.headline: Putin's domestic approval ratings fell for seven consecutive weeks as of 24 April 2026, with state pollster VTsIOM recording United Russia support at 27.7%, driven by internet crackdowns and economic deterioration.
open_findings.why_it_matters: Sustained domestic approval decline in Russia — if it continues — creates internal political pressure that could either constrain or accelerate Putin's war decision-making; historically, Russian leaders have used external escalation to manage domestic legitimacy crises.

open_findings.headline: Iran struck Gulf energy infrastructure across multiple states during the US-Israeli conflict, with projections suggesting Qatar and Kuwait could face GDP contractions of up to 14% and Saudi Arabia and UAE declines of 3-5% if the conflict had continued through April.
open_findings.why_it_matters: The economic shock potential of the Iran conflict on Gulf state GDP — even partially realized — represents a structural shift in Gulf security calculus that will shape Saudi-UAE-Iran relations and Sudan proxy war dynamics for the remainder of 2026.

reverse_cascade_check.geopolitical_event: US-Iran 8 April ceasefire and partial Gulf de-escalation
reverse_cascade_check.affected_boundary: JCPOA successor / Iran nuclear diplomacy framework
reverse_cascade_check.cascade_description: The 8 April US-Iran ceasefire, if it holds and deepens, creates a narrow window for nuclear diplomacy that did not exist during active hostilities; however, Crisis Group assesses this window has already narrowed significantly because the administration failed to establish red lines in advance, and Iran's post-ceasefire strikes on UAE on 4-5 May signal the framework is fragile. The ceasefire could reverse the Iran regional escalation trajectory but simultaneously risks locking in a no-deal nuclear status quo that is more dangerous than the pre-conflict baseline.
reverse_cascade_check.note: The 8 April US-Iran ceasefire is the only event this cycle with reverse-cascade potential, but its fragility — demonstrated by Iran's 4-5 May UAE strikes — and the absence of a nuclear diplomacy framework mean the de-escalation boundary shift is conditional and may not materialize; analysts should monitor whether the ceasefire produces any formal nuclear talks channel within the next 30 days as the key threshold indicator.
